text: This video highlights the importance of using a circular anal dilator to both diagnose and guide fixation of internal rectal prolapse during rectal prolapse surgery, getting it right first time. Internal rectal prolapse is full thickness interception of the rectum. Whilst it can occur in normal individuals, it has been shown to be morphologically more advanced in symptomatic patients with more high-grade or rectoanal interception present.

Patients are usually seen in the pelvic floor clinic and then we'll have specialist investigations. While some centers use conventional x-ray dynamic defecating proctography, we use MRI, which provides higher quality images of the pelvic floor and viscera. This sequence of MRI images shows a patient with ODS due to a high-grade recto-anal interception. MRI also allows accurate management of all three pelvic floor compartments.

However, whilst providing accurate images, MRI can both understage and also fail to diagnose rectal interception. We have found this looking at the data from our own unit. And thus we seek to complement cases of suspected IRP with an EUA. A clear circular anal dilator, such as the one shown here, is a very accurate way of demonstrating an internal rectal prolapse and also assessing its grade. we use the Oxford grading system. It overcomes a limitation of proctography and also allows anterior or posterior predominance to be noted.
The surgeon uses a circular anal dilator during the corrective surgery for internal rectal prolapse to elevate the rectum out of the pelvis, ensuring the prolapse is fully reduced before fixation.

The first patient is a sixty-year-old lady with severe ODS but fortunately without concomitant faecal incontinence. Her risk factors for pelvic organ prolapse include three previous vaginal deliveries and also a hysterectomy. MRDP whilst confirming a full thickness rectal interception showed this only to be intra-arrectal. MRI also identified pelvic floor descent and a rectocele. A transit study showed no evidence of slow transit constipation.

Treatment was initiated with correct positioning during defecation laxatives and pelvic floor physiotherapy. Despite these, her symptoms persisted and the patient requested surgery. At EUA, an Oxford Grade III IRP was found and due to significant sigmoid pathology, an attempt at VMR was abandoned. She was subsequently rediscussed and offered resection rectopexy. A second EUA had identified progression of the degree of IRP, which was now Grade IV posteriorly.

The surgeon mobilizes the redundant sigmoid colon back to the embryological midline, followed by mobilization of the rectum down to the pelvic floor in the TME-HODY plane. After resecting the sigmoid colon, a stapled colorectal anastomosis is performed, confirmed to be airtight and well-perfused via endoscopy.

Three months following the procedure, the patient reports improved stool frequency and her ODS symptoms have improved. She is very satisfied with the outcome of surgery but longer term data is awaited.

In our second patient, we again demonstrate the value of the CAD to help guide correction of internal rectal prolapse. This patient is a seventy three year old lady with long standing ODS who has started to now also experience faecal incontinence. She had significant risk factors for pelvic organ prolapse and pelvic floor weakness. A full pelvic floor workup confirmed high-grade rectal interception on MRDP with normal sphincter pressures at manometry. She had anatomically intact anal sphincters on endoanal ultrasound scan, but a thickened internal anal sphincter, which is not uncommonly seen in IRP.

During the laparoscopic procedure, the surgeon identifies severe diverticular disease with the sigmoid colon adherent to the peritoneum over the vaginal vault. The examination under anesthesia confirms a grade four internal rectal prolapse.

The surgeon frees the sigmoid colon from the vaginal vault, revealing a deep and wide pelvis. After dividing the inferior mesenteric artery, the rectum is fully mobilized in the TME plane down to the pelvic floor. The sigmoid colon is resected, and a stapled side-to-end anastomosis is performed, confirmed to be airtight and well-perfused on flexible sigmoidoscopy.

When reviewed at twelve months post-surgery, the patient was considerably better with no further incontinence episodes and also resolution of the ADF symptoms, delighted with her outcome. In conclusion, we believe that the management of all patients with internal rectal prolapse ought to always commence conservatively, using the salt-to-cooking mantra. In cases that fail conservative management, we advocate discussion in a pelvic floor MDT, And in those select cases deemed appropriate for surgery, we strongly recommend the use of a circular anal dilator to ensure complete anatomical reduction of the prolapse. Thank you for listening.
